syllabus_desc: Describe the requirements to hold an aviation document, as laid down in CA Act 2023 S73.
concept: To get any aviation document in New Zealand — whether that's a pilot licence, an air operator certificate, or an aircraft airworthiness certificate — you need to apply to the Director of Civil Aviation under section 73 of the Civil Aviation Act 2023. The Director will grant or renew the document if you meet the prescribed requirements, are a fit and proper person, and the grant isn't contrary to the interests of aviation safety or security (s75). Your aviation document can come with conditions, and it stays valid for the period specified on it (s78).
exam_tip: The three-part test for granting an aviation document comes up regularly: (1) prescribed requirements met, (2) fit and proper person, (3) not contrary to safety/security. Know all three.
key_facts: Apply to the Director under CA Act 2023 s73
Three conditions for grant: prescribed requirements, fit and proper, not contrary to safety/security (s75)
Documents may have conditions attached and have a specified validity period (s78)
Right of appeal to the District Court if refused (s79)
source: Civil Aviation Act 2023 ss73, 75, 78, 79; AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: Describe the criteria for the fit and proper person test, as laid down in CA Act 2023 S80.
concept: The 'fit and proper person' test is the Director's way of deciding whether you're someone who should hold an aviation document. Under section 80 of the CA Act 2023, the Director looks at your compliance history with transport legislation, your experience and qualifications, your knowledge, your physical and mental health (including drug and alcohol use), any relevant criminal convictions, and any other matters the Director considers relevant to aviation safety. It's a broad test — the Director has significant discretion.
exam_tip: Remember the key criteria: compliance history, experience, qualifications, knowledge, health (drugs/alcohol), and criminal convictions. The Director can also consider 'any other matter' relevant to safety.
key_facts: Compliance history with transport legislation
Experience, qualifications, and knowledge
Physical and mental health, including drugs and alcohol
Criminal convictions relevant to fitness to hold the document
Any other matter the Director considers relevant to aviation safety
source: Civil Aviation Act 2023 s80; AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: Describe the duties of the pilot-in-command, as laid down in CA Act 2023 S14 and S13.
concept: As PIC, you carry a lot of responsibility. Under section 14, you're responsible for the safe operation of the aircraft, the safety of all passengers, crew, and cargo during flight time, and compliance with all relevant aviation rules. You're the final authority on the aircraft — if something needs deciding in the air, it's your call. Section 13 adds the general requirement that every aviation participant must carry out their activities safely and in accordance with the relevant rules.
exam_tip: The PIC is the 'final authority' on the aircraft — that phrase appears often in exam questions. Don't confuse PIC duties (s14) with general participant duties (s13).
key_facts: PIC is responsible for safe operation of the aircraft (s14)
Responsible for safety of passengers, crew, and cargo during flight time
PIC is the final authority on the aircraft
Must comply with all relevant Civil Aviation Rules
General duty: all aviation participants must operate safely (s13)
source: Civil Aviation Act 2023 ss13, 14; AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: Describe the responsibilities of a licence holder with respect to changes in their medical condition, as laid down in CA Act 2023.
concept: If your medical condition changes in a way that could affect your ability to fly safely, you have a legal obligation to deal with it. Under the Act and associated rules, you must not exercise the privileges of your licence if you know of any medical condition that would prevent you meeting the requirements for your medical certificate. You're expected to ground yourself if in doubt — and notify your medical examiner or the CAA of significant changes. This isn't just about formal conditions — if you've got the flu and you're not fit to fly, don't fly.
exam_tip: The obligation is on YOU as the licence holder to self-assess. If in doubt about your medical fitness, the safe and legal answer is always: don't fly until cleared.
key_facts: Must not exercise licence privileges if aware of a medical condition affecting safety
Obligation to notify changes in medical condition
Self-assessment responsibility rests with the licence holder
Must surrender medical certificate if no longer meeting requirements
Acting while medically unfit is an offence under s110
source: Civil Aviation Act 2023 ss109, 110, Schedule 2; AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: Describe the responsibilities of a licence holder with respect to the surrender of a medical certificate, as laid down in CA Act 2023.
concept: If you no longer meet the requirements for your medical certificate, you must surrender it. You can't just tuck it in a drawer and keep flying — the Act requires you to return it to the CAA. The Director also has powers to suspend or impose conditions on your medical certificate if there's reason to believe you no longer meet the medical standard. Failing to surrender when required is an offence.
exam_tip: Surrender isn't optional — if you know you don't meet the medical standard, you must hand it back. The Director can also demand surrender through suspension powers.
key_facts: Must surrender medical certificate if no longer meeting requirements
Director may suspend or impose conditions on medical certificates (s96)
Failure to surrender when required is an offence
The obligation is triggered by the holder's own knowledge of their condition
Director's suspension powers take effect immediately but expire after 10 working days unless extended (s97)
source: Civil Aviation Act 2023 ss96, 97, 109, 110; AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: Describe the responsibilities of a licence holder with respect to safety offences, as laid down in CA Act 2023 S40 and S41.
concept: The Act takes safety offences very seriously. Section 40 covers operating an aircraft carelessly — that is, without reasonable care or in a manner that could endanger people or property. The penalties are up to $30,000 for individuals or $100,000 for organisations. Section 41 is the big one: performing any dangerous aviation activity. If your actions cause unnecessary danger to people or property, you're looking at fines up to $150,000 (or $1.5M for organisations) under subsection 2, and for the most serious cases involving wilful or reckless conduct, up to 5 years' imprisonment plus $300,000 (or $3M for organisations) under subsection 3.
exam_tip: Know the difference: s40 is 'careless' ($30k/$100k), s41 is 'dangerous' ($150k/$1.5M or 5 years + $300k/$3M). The prison time only applies to the most serious s41(3) offences.
key_facts: S40: Operating carelessly — up to $30,000 individual / $100,000 organisation
S41(2): Dangerous activity — up to $150,000 / $1,500,000
S41(3): Wilful/reckless dangerous activity — up to 5 years prison + $300,000 / $3,000,000
S42: Operating in controlled/restricted airspace without authorisation is also an offence
S43: Communicating false information relating to aviation safety is an offence
source: Civil Aviation Act 2023 ss40, 41, 42, 43; AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: accident, aerobatic flight, controlled flight, cross-country flight, cost sharing flight, VFR flight.
concept:
Here are the key flight-related definitions from CAR Part 1 you need to know:

• Accident — has the meaning given in section 6 of the Act (broadly: an occurrence involving an aircraft where a person is fatally or seriously injured, the aircraft sustains substantial damage, or the aircraft is missing).
• Aerobatic flight — (1) an intentional manoeuvre where the aircraft is in sustained inverted flight or rolled from upright to inverted; or (2) manoeuvres like rolls, loops, spins, upward vertical flight culminating in a stall turn, hammerhead or whip stall, or a combination.
• Controlled flight — has the meaning given in section 5 of the Act.
• Cross-country flight — a flight extending more than 25 NM in a straight line from the centre of the aerodrome of departure.
• Cost sharing flight — defined under rule 91.227B.
• VFR flight — a flight conducted in accordance with the visual flight rules.
exam_tip: Cross-country = more than 25 NM from the departure aerodrome (straight line). Aerobatic flight covers any sustained inverted flight OR rolls, loops, spins, and stall turns.
key_facts: Cross-country flight: more than 25 NM straight line from departure aerodrome centre
Aerobatic flight: sustained inverted flight, or rolls/loops/spins/stall turns/hammerheads
VFR flight: conducted in accordance with visual flight rules
Accident: defined in s6 of the Act — fatal/serious injury, substantial damage, or missing aircraft

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: aerodrome, aerodrome elevation, aerodrome operational area, aerodrome traffic circuit, landing area, apron.
concept:
Your aerodrome-related definitions:

• Aerodrome — has the meaning given in section 5 of the Act (any defined area of land or water used for aircraft operations).
• Aerodrome elevation — the elevation of the highest point of the landing area (AIP GEN).
• Aerodrome operational area — the movement area plus any ground installations or facilities provided for the safety of aircraft operations, and includes areas designated by signs as operational areas.
• Aerodrome traffic circuit — has the meaning given in section 5 of the Act.
• Landing area — that part of a movement area intended for the landing or take-off of aircraft.
• Apron — a defined area on a land aerodrome intended to accommodate aircraft for loading/unloading passengers or cargo, refuelling, parking, or maintenance.
exam_tip: Don't confuse aerodrome elevation (highest point of the landing area) with elevation generally (vertical distance from MSL). The operational area is bigger than just the movement area — it includes safety installations.
key_facts: Aerodrome elevation: highest point of the landing area
Landing area: part of the movement area for landing/take-off
Apron: area for loading, unloading, refuelling, parking, maintenance
Operational area: movement area + safety installations + signed areas
Movement area: part used for take-off, landing, and surface movement (includes manoeuvring area, maintenance areas, and aprons)
source: CAR Part 1 rule 1.1; AIP GEN; AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: pilot-in-command, passenger, fit and proper person, rating, alerting service, ATC service, flight information service.
concept:
Key people and service definitions:

• Pilot-in-command — has the meaning given in section 5 of the Act (the pilot designated as being in command and responsible for the safe conduct of the flight).
• Passenger — has the meaning given in section 5 of the Act (a person carried on an aircraft other than a crew member).
• Fit and proper person — a person who satisfies the fit and proper person test specified in the Act (s80 criteria).
• Rating — an authorisation entered on or associated with a licence, certificate, or logbook, stating special conditions, privileges, or limitations.
• Alerting service — has the meaning given in section 5 of the Act.
• Air traffic control (ATC) service — has the meaning given in section 5 of the Act.
• Flight information service — has the meaning given in section 5 of the Act.
exam_tip: A rating isn't a separate licence — it's an authorisation attached to your licence that expands or limits what you can do. Common PPL ratings include type ratings and night ratings.
key_facts: PIC: the pilot designated as in command and responsible for safe conduct of flight
Passenger: a person carried on the aircraft who is not a crew member
Rating: an authorisation on a licence stating special conditions, privileges, or limitations
Fit and proper person: satisfies the test in s80 of the Act
ATC service, alerting service, and FIS are all defined in the Act
source: CAR Part 1 rule 1.1; CA Act 2023 s5; AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: altitude, height, ceiling, day, night, visibility, visual meteorological conditions, visual reference.
concept:
Your measurement and weather definitions — and getting these right matters:

• Altitude — the vertical distance of a level, point, or object measured from mean sea level (MSL).
• Height — the vertical distance of a level, point, or object measured from a specified datum, and includes the vertical dimension of an object.
• Ceiling — the height above ground or water of the base of the lowest layer of cloud below 20,000 ft covering more than half the sky.
• Day — the hours between the beginning of morning civil twilight and the end of evening civil twilight (sun's centre 6° below horizon).
• Night — the hours between the end of evening civil twilight and the beginning of morning civil twilight.
• Visibility — the ability to see and identify prominent unlighted objects by day and prominent lighted objects by night.
• Visual meteorological conditions (VMC) — conditions expressed in terms of visibility, distance from cloud, and ceiling equal to or better than specified minima.
• Visual reference — AIP GEN definition.
exam_tip: Altitude is from MSL, height is from a specified datum (often the ground). Civil twilight = sun's centre 6° below horizon. Ceiling only counts cloud covering MORE than half the sky below 20,000 ft.
key_facts: Altitude: vertical distance from mean sea level (MSL)
Height: vertical distance from a specified datum (includes object dimensions)
Ceiling: base of lowest cloud layer covering >50% sky, below 20,000 ft AGL
Day/Night: defined by civil twilight (sun centre 6° below horizon)
VMC: visibility, cloud distance, and ceiling meeting or exceeding specified minima

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: aeronautical information circular, aeronautical information publication (AIP), AIP supplement, NOTAM, basic weather report, AWIB service.
concept:
Your aviation publications and information definitions:

• Aeronautical Information Circular (AIC) — a notice containing information that doesn't qualify for NOTAM or AIP inclusion, but relates to flight safety, air navigation, technical, admin, or legislative matters.
• Aeronautical Information Publication (AIP) — a publication issued by or with the authority of a State, containing aeronautical information of a lasting character essential to air navigation.
• AIP Supplement — temporary changes to AIP information published on special pages.
• NOTAM — a notice distributed by telecommunication containing information about any aeronautical facility, service, procedure, or hazard, the timely knowledge of which is essential for flight operations.
• Basic weather report — a verbal comment describing current weather: wind, pressure, temperature, and cloud/conditions.
• AWIB Service — an automatic broadcast of aerodrome and weather information for aviation. Not an ATS service.
exam_tip: NOTAMs are for urgent/temporary info distributed electronically. AIP Supplements are also temporary but published on special pages. AICs are for longer-term non-urgent info. AWIB is NOT an air traffic service.
key_facts: NOTAM: urgent info distributed by telecomms, essential for flight operations
AIP: lasting aeronautical information essential to air navigation
AIP Supplement: temporary changes published on special pages
AIC: non-urgent info that doesn't qualify for NOTAM or AIP
AWIB: automatic broadcast of aerodrome/weather info — NOT an ATS

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: airworthiness certificate, airworthy condition, aviation event, dangerous goods, emergency locator transmitter, personal locator beacon, takeoff weight, type.
concept:
Aircraft and equipment definitions:

• Airworthiness certificate — for NZ aircraft, a certificate issued by the Director under Part 21; for foreign aircraft, issued by the competent authority of the State of registry.
• Airworthy condition — the condition of an aircraft (including components, fuel, and materials) that complies with all requirements prescribed by the CARs for design, manufacture, maintenance, modification, repair, and safety.
• Aviation event — an event conducted below minimum safe heights: air shows, air races, aerobatic competitions, or aerobatic training/practice.
• Dangerous goods — has the meaning given in section 5 of the Act.
• Emergency locator transmitter (ELT) — equipment that broadcasts a distinctive signal on a designated frequency to facilitate search and rescue.
• Personal locator beacon (PLB) — equipment broadcasting a signal on a designated frequency to help search and rescue, designed to be carried on a person, manually activated.
• Takeoff weight — the weight of the aeroplane at the commencement of the take-off run, including everything and everyone on board.
• Type — for licensing purposes, all aircraft of the same basic design including modifications that don't significantly change handling or flight characteristics.
exam_tip: ELT is installed in the aircraft; PLB is carried on a person. Both broadcast on designated SAR frequencies but they serve different roles. Takeoff weight = everything on the aircraft at the start of the take-off run.
key_facts: Airworthy condition: complies with ALL CAR requirements for design, manufacture, maintenance, repair, safety
Aviation event: conducted below minimum safe heights (shows, races, aerobatics)
ELT: installed in aircraft, broadcasts SAR signal
PLB: carried on a person, manually activated, broadcasts SAR signal
Takeoff weight: total weight at commencement of take-off run
Type: aircraft of same basic design (for licensing purposes)

syllabus_desc: State the definition of: Act, ATC clearance, ATC instruction, dual flight time, final reserve fuel, flight manual, flight plan, flight time, incident, SARTIME, simultaneous operations, Technical Instructions, UNICOM service.
concept:
More definitions you'll need:

• Act — the Civil Aviation Act 2023.
• ATC clearance — authorisation for an aircraft to proceed under conditions specified by an ATC unit.
• ATC instruction — a directive issued by ATC requiring a pilot to take a specific action.
• Dual flight time — flight time during which a person is receiving instruction from an appropriately licensed instructor in a dual-control aircraft.
• Final reserve fuel — the minimum fuel to provide a margin for safe completion of a flight in the event of unplanned manoeuvring near the destination.
• Flight manual — a manual associated with the certificate of airworthiness, containing limitations and instructions for safe operation.
• Flight plan — specified information to be provided to an ATS unit or flight following service regarding an intended flight.
• Flight time — for an aircraft (other than a balloon), the total time from when the aircraft first moves for the purpose of flight until it comes to rest at the end, including push back, taxiing, and holding.
• Incident — has the meaning given in section 5 of the Act.
• SARTIME — the time nominated by a pilot for the initiation of alerting action.
• Simultaneous operations — AIP GEN definition.
• Technical Instructions — ICAO Doc 9284, Technical Instructions for the Safe Transport of Dangerous Goods by Air.
• UNICOM service — a ground radio service providing local aerodrome information for aviation. NOT an air traffic service.
exam_tip: Flight time starts when the aircraft first MOVES for the purpose of flight (not just engine start) and ends when it comes to rest. SARTIME is what YOU nominate — if you don't cancel your flight plan before SARTIME, search and rescue will be initiated.
key_facts: ATC clearance: authorisation to proceed; ATC instruction: directive to take specific action
Flight time: from first movement for flight to coming to rest (includes taxi/holding)
SARTIME: pilot-nominated time for initiation of alerting action
Final reserve fuel: minimum fuel margin for safe completion after unplanned manoeuvring
UNICOM: ground radio providing aerodrome info — NOT an air traffic service
Act = Civil Aviation Act 2023

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the following abbreviations: ABN, AGL, AFIS, AFRU, AMSL, ATIS, AWIB, AWS, BWR, CAR, DTHR, ECT, ELT.
concept:
Here are the abbreviations from CAR Part 1 and AIP GEN you need to know (first group):

• ABN — Aerodrome beacon (AIP GEN)
• AGL — Above ground level
• AFIS — Aerodrome flight information service (AIP GEN)
• AFRU — Aerodrome Frequency Response Unit
• AMSL — Above mean sea level
• ATIS — Automatic terminal information service
• AWIB — Aerodrome and Weather Information Broadcast
• AWS — Automatic weather station (AIP GEN)
• BWR — Basic weather report (AIP GEN)
• CAR — Civil Aviation Rules
• DTHR — Displaced threshold (AIP GEN)
• ECT — Estimated completion time (AIP GEN)
• ELT — Emergency locator transmitter
exam_tip: AFRU is the automatic 'parrot' that gives you a tone or voice confirmation of your radio frequency — it's NOT a manned service. AFIS is a flight information service at an aerodrome. Know the difference.
key_facts: AGL: above ground level; AMSL: above mean sea level
ATIS: automatic terminal information service
AFRU: Aerodrome Frequency Response Unit (automatic confirmation)
AFIS: aerodrome flight information service
ELT: emergency locator transmitter
CAR: Civil Aviation Rules
source: CAR Part 1 rule 1.3; AIP GEN; AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the following abbreviations: FATO, ME1, ME2, MCT, POB, QNH, TALO, TLOF, VFR, VPC.
concept:
More abbreviations (note: FATO, TALO, TLOF are helicopter-only but may still appear):

• FATO — Final approach and take-off area (helicopter, AIP GEN)
• ME1 — Medical Examiner 1 Certificate holder
• ME2 — Medical Examiner 2 Certificate holder
• MCT — Minimum crossing time (AIP GEN)
• POB — Persons on board (AIP GEN)
• QNH — Altimeter sub-scale setting to obtain elevation when on the ground
• TALO — Take-off and landing area operations (helicopter, AIP GEN)
• TLOF — Touchdown and lift-off area (helicopter, AIP GEN)
• VFR — Visual flight rules
• VPC — Visual position circle (AIP GEN)
exam_tip: QNH gives you elevation on the ground and altitude in flight. ME1 and ME2 refer to medical examiners who hold different level certificates — ME1 can issue Class 1 medicals, ME2 can issue Class 2.
key_facts: QNH: altimeter setting to obtain elevation on the ground
VFR: visual flight rules
ME1/ME2: Medical Examiner Certificate holders (levels 1 and 2)
POB: persons on board
FATO/TALO/TLOF: helicopter-specific aerodrome terms
source: CAR Part 1 rule 1.3; AIP GEN; AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for holding a pilot's licence. CAR 61.
concept: To hold a pilot's licence in New Zealand, you need to meet the requirements in Part 61 of the Civil Aviation Rules. At the most basic level, you need to be the minimum age (17 for a PPL), hold the appropriate medical certificate (Class 2 for PPL), have completed the required flight experience, passed the written examinations, and passed a flight test. Your licence must remain current — which means keeping your medical current, meeting recent experience requirements, and completing biennial flight reviews.
exam_tip: Holding a licence and exercising its privileges are two different things. You can hold an expired licence, but you can't fly on it until you've renewed everything.
key_facts: Must meet minimum age requirement (17 for PPL)
Must hold appropriate medical certificate
Must complete required flight experience hours
Must pass written examinations and flight test
Must maintain currency: medical, recent experience, BFR

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for a PIC to hold a type rating on the type of aircraft being flown. CAR 61.
concept: Before you can fly as PIC on a particular type of aircraft, you need to hold a type rating for that type. Under Part 61, you need to demonstrate competency on the specific aircraft type to a flight instructor or examiner. A 'type' means all aircraft of the same basic design — so small modifications don't create a new type, but significant changes to handling or flight characteristics do. Your type rating is endorsed on your licence or logbook.
exam_tip: You can't just jump into any aeroplane with your PPL — you need a type rating for each type you fly as PIC.
key_facts: PIC must hold a type rating for the aircraft type being flown
Type = aircraft of same basic design (excluding minor modifications)
Must demonstrate competency to an instructor or examiner
Type rating is endorsed on licence or logbook
Significant changes in handling/flight characteristics = different type
source: CAR Part 61; CAR Part 1 (type definition); AC 61-3 Rev 25

syllabus_desc: State the general requirements for entering flight details into a pilot's logbook. CAR 61.
concept: Your pilot's logbook is a legal record of your flight experience. Under Part 61, you must record your flights accurately, including the date, aircraft type and registration, departure and destination points, flight time, and the nature of the flight (solo, dual, PIC, etc.). Logbook entries must be made in a form acceptable to the Director. Accurate logbook keeping isn't just bureaucracy — it's how you prove your experience for licence issue, rating issue, and currency.
exam_tip: Your logbook is a legal document. Falsifying entries is a serious offence. Keep it accurate and up to date.
key_facts: Must record: date, aircraft type/registration, departure/destination, flight time, nature of flight
Entries must be accurate and in a form acceptable to the Director
Used to prove experience for licence/rating issue and currency
Logbook is a legal document — falsification is an offence

syllabus_desc: Describe the allowance for a person who does not hold a current pilot licence to fly dual with a flying instructor. CAR 61.
concept: You don't need a pilot licence to receive flight instruction. A student pilot can fly dual (with a qualified flight instructor in the other seat) without holding a licence. That's how everyone starts — you can't get a licence without first learning to fly, and you can't learn to fly without being in an aircraft. The instructor holds the responsibility during dual instruction, and the aircraft must have fully functioning dual controls.
exam_tip: No licence needed for dual instruction — but solo flight requires meeting specific requirements under Part 61.
key_facts: No pilot licence required to fly dual with a qualified instructor
Aircraft must have fully functioning dual controls
The instructor is responsible during dual instruction
This is how all pilot training begins

syllabus_desc: State the solo flight requirements on a person who does not hold a current pilot licence. CAR 61.
concept: To fly solo without a full pilot licence, you need to be a student pilot who has been authorised to fly solo by your flight instructor. Your instructor must be satisfied you're competent to safely conduct the solo flight, and you must be operating within the limitations and conditions they've set. You also need to hold a current medical certificate (Class 2 minimum) and carry appropriate identification.
exam_tip: Solo authority comes from your instructor — they determine when you're ready and can set limitations on what you can do solo.
key_facts: Must be authorised for solo by a qualified flight instructor
Instructor must be satisfied of competency for the specific flight
Must hold current medical certificate (minimum Class 2)
Must operate within instructor-imposed limitations
Must carry appropriate identification

syllabus_desc: State the limitations on a person who does not hold a current pilot licence. CAR 61.
concept: A student pilot operating solo is under significant limitations. You can't carry passengers. You can't fly for hire or reward. You must stay within the conditions and limitations set by your instructor — which might restrict you to specific aerodromes, routes, weather conditions, or times of day. You're essentially operating under your instructor's supervision even though they're not in the aircraft.
exam_tip: The key limitation: NO passengers when flying solo as a student. Your instructor's limitations are legally binding.
key_facts: Cannot carry passengers
Cannot fly for hire or reward
Must stay within instructor-imposed limitations
May be restricted to specific aerodromes, routes, weather, times
Must hold current medical certificate

syllabus_desc: State the eligibility requirements for the issue of a private pilot licence. CAR 61.
concept: Under rule 61.153, to get a PPL (Aeroplane) you need to: be at least 17 years old; hold a current Class 2 medical certificate; have at least 50 hours total flight time (or 40 hours if you're not including cross-country experience in the total); have completed the required cross-country and night experience (if you want those privileges); have passed the written examinations; and have passed a flight test with a flight examiner.
exam_tip: Minimum age 17, minimum 50 hours (or 40 without cross-country). Class 2 medical. Written exams AND flight test required.
key_facts: Minimum age: 17 years
Medical: current Class 2 medical certificate
Experience: minimum 50 hours (or 40 hours without cross-country)
Must pass written examinations for all required subjects
Must pass a flight test with a flight examiner
Night and cross-country experience required for those privileges

syllabus_desc: State the privileges of holding a private pilot licence. CAR 61.
concept: With a PPL under rule 61.155, you can act as pilot-in-command of an aircraft of the appropriate category, carry passengers, and act as co-pilot. Those are your core privileges. But they come with strings — you can only exercise them if you're current, and certain privileges (like night flying, cross-country, sling loads) require that you've been certified as competent by an instructor in those areas.
exam_tip: PPL = PIC + carry passengers + co-pilot. But NOT for hire or reward (that's the big limitation).
key_facts: May act as PIC of an aircraft of the appropriate category
May carry passengers
May act as co-pilot
Specific privileges (night, cross-country, sling) require instructor certification
Must be current to exercise any privilege

syllabus_desc: State the limitations on the holder of a private pilot licence. CAR 61.
concept: The PPL comes with clear limitations under rule 61.155. You must not fly for remuneration. You must not fly for hire or reward. You must not exercise privileges at night, on cross-country flights, or for sling load operations unless you've been certified competent in those areas by a flight instructor. If you hold a lower-class medical certificate, there may be additional limitations on your privileges (rule 61.155(bb)).
exam_tip: The golden rule: PPL holders cannot fly for remuneration or hire/reward. That's what separates private from commercial. Cost-sharing is the one exception — and it has strict rules.
key_facts: Must NOT fly for remuneration
Must NOT fly for hire or reward
Night flying requires instructor certification of competency
Cross-country requires instructor certification of competency
Sling load operations require instructor certification
Lower medical certificates may impose additional limitations

syllabus_desc: State the requirements and limitations of a PPL holder sharing the cost of a flight. CAR 1.
concept: Cost sharing is the exception that lets PPL holders split flight costs with passengers — but it's tightly controlled. Under the cost sharing rules (defined in Part 1 and rule 91.227B), you can share costs with your passengers, but only if the flight has a bona fide purpose other than just going flying, you share the costs equally, and you don't profit from the arrangement. You're essentially splitting the cost of a trip you were going to make anyway — not running a taxi service.
exam_tip: Cost sharing ≠ hire or reward. You must share costs equally, the flight must have a genuine purpose, and you must not profit. If you're only making the flight because someone wants a ride, that's hire or reward.
key_facts: Costs must be shared equally among all persons on the flight
The flight must have a bona fide purpose beyond just the flight itself
The PPL holder must not profit from the arrangement
Defined in CAR Part 1 and rule 91.227B
Cost sharing is NOT hire or reward when rules are followed

syllabus_desc: State the recent experience requirements of a PIC who is the holder of a PPL, by day and by night. CAR 61.
concept: Under rule 61.37, to act as PIC carrying passengers, you need recent experience. For day flying, you must have completed at least 3 take-offs and 3 landings as PIC or co-pilot within the preceding 90 days in an aircraft of the same category. For night flying, you need the same — 3 take-offs and 3 landings at night within the preceding 90 days. If you haven't met these requirements, you can still fly solo to regain currency, but you can't carry passengers until you do.
exam_tip: 3 take-offs and 3 landings in 90 days — same category aircraft. Day currency doesn't count for night, and vice versa. You can fly solo to regain currency.
key_facts: Day: 3 take-offs and 3 landings as PIC/co-pilot within preceding 90 days
Night: 3 take-offs and 3 landings at night within preceding 90 days
Must be in an aircraft of the same category
Required to carry passengers — not required for solo flight
Can fly solo to regain currency

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for the completion of a biennial flight review. CAR 61.
concept: Under rule 61.39, every pilot must complete a biennial flight review (BFR) at least once every 24 months to exercise PIC privileges. The BFR is conducted by a flight instructor or examiner and covers both the practical flying skills and the aviation knowledge you need for safe operations. Think of it as a periodic competency check — not an exam you pass or fail, but a review where the instructor confirms you're still operating safely and identifies areas for improvement.
exam_tip: BFR = every 24 months. It's a review, not a test — but your instructor must be satisfied of your competency before signing you off.
key_facts: Required every 24 months to exercise PIC privileges
Conducted by a flight instructor or flight examiner
Covers practical flying skills and aviation knowledge
Instructor/examiner must be satisfied of competency
Completing a flight test can reset the BFR clock

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for holding a medical certificate. CAR 61.
concept: Under rule 61.35, you must hold a current and valid medical certificate appropriate to your licence to exercise any flying privileges. For a PPL, the minimum is a Class 2 medical certificate. Your medical must be issued by a designated medical examiner (ME1 or ME2), and it must be current — which means within its validity period and not suspended or revoked.
exam_tip: No current medical = no flying. A Class 2 medical is the minimum for PPL. The medical requirement is one of the three currency requirements (medical, recent experience, BFR).
key_facts: Must hold current and valid medical certificate to exercise flying privileges
PPL minimum: Class 2 medical certificate
Issued by a designated medical examiner (ME1 or ME2)
Must be within validity period and not suspended/revoked
Part of the three currency requirements: medical, recent experience, BFR

syllabus_desc: State the requirements on a person applying for a medical certificate. CAR 67.
concept: When you apply for a medical certificate under Part 67, you must undergo a medical examination by a designated aviation medical examiner (DAME). You need to provide a complete and accurate medical history, disclose any known conditions or medications, and undergo the examinations specified for your class of medical. Being honest on your medical application isn't optional — providing false or misleading information is an offence.
exam_tip: Full disclosure is required. Hiding a medical condition doesn't make it go away — and if it's discovered later, you could lose your licence entirely.
key_facts: Must undergo examination by a designated aviation medical examiner
Must provide complete and accurate medical history
Must disclose all known conditions and medications
False or misleading information is an offence
Examination requirements vary by class of medical

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for maintaining medical fitness following the issue of a medical certificate. CA Act 2023.
concept: Your medical certificate represents your fitness at the time of issue — but the obligation to be medically fit is ongoing. Between medicals, if your health changes in a way that might affect your ability to fly safely, you must ground yourself and get it checked out. The Act (Schedule 2) and rules require you to maintain the medical standard continuously, not just at your medical examinations.
exam_tip: The medical standard is CONTINUOUS, not just at renewal time. If you break your arm the day after your medical, you can't fly until cleared — even though your medical certificate is technically current.
key_facts: Medical fitness is a continuous obligation, not just at examination time
Must self-assess fitness before each flight
Must ground yourself if health changes affect ability to fly safely
Must notify changes to medical examiner or CAA
Acting while medically unfit is an offence (CA Act 2023 s110)

syllabus_desc: State the normal currency period of the Class 2 medical certificate for a PPL holder who is under the age of 40. CAR 67.
concept: For PPL holders under 40, a Class 2 medical certificate is valid for 5 years. That's the standard validity period. Once you turn 40, it shortens. The idea is that younger pilots generally have fewer emerging health issues, so less frequent checking is needed — but remember, the continuous fitness obligation still applies regardless of your medical's validity period.
exam_tip: Under 40 = 5-year Class 2 medical. Simple as that. But watch out — if you develop any condition in between, you must self-ground.
key_facts: Class 2 medical for PPL holder under 40: valid for 5 years
Continuous fitness obligation still applies between examinations
Must self-assess before each flight regardless of medical currency

syllabus_desc: State the normal currency period of the Class 2 medical certificate for a PPL holder who is 40 years or more on the date the certificate is issued. CAR 67.
concept: Once you're 40 or over, your Class 2 medical certificate validity shortens to 2 years. The shorter period reflects the increased likelihood of developing conditions that could affect flight safety as you get older. Like the under-40 certificate, you still have the continuous obligation to self-assess and ground yourself if your medical condition changes between examinations.
exam_tip: 40 and over = 2-year Class 2 medical. Under 40 = 5 years. These are common exam questions.
key_facts: Class 2 medical for PPL holder aged 40+: valid for 2 years
Shorter period due to increased health risks with age
Same continuous fitness obligation applies
Must self-ground if medical condition changes

syllabus_desc: State the documents which must be carried in aircraft operated in New Zealand. CAR 91.
concept: Under rule 91.111, every aircraft operating in New Zealand must carry: the current airworthiness certificate (or equivalent), the aircraft flight manual (or equivalent operating limitations), the current technical log (maintenance release), current weight and balance information, and the radio station approval form (if fitted with a radio). These documents must be accessible in the aircraft — not sitting in a filing cabinet back at the hangar.
exam_tip: Remember the five documents: airworthiness certificate, flight manual, tech log (maintenance release), weight and balance, and radio form. Daily flight records must be retained for 12 months (rule 91.112).
key_facts: Current airworthiness certificate
Aircraft flight manual (or equivalent operating limitations)
Current technical log / maintenance release
Current weight and balance information
Radio station approval form (if radio fitted)
Daily flight records retained for 12 months (rule 91.112)

syllabus_desc: Describe the maintenance requirements of an aircraft operator. CAR 91.
concept: As an operator, you're responsible for ensuring your aircraft is maintained in an airworthy condition. The aircraft must be maintained in accordance with its approved maintenance programme, and all maintenance must be carried out by appropriately certificated persons or organisations. You can't just fly it until something breaks — there's a structured programme of inspections and maintenance that keeps the aircraft safe.
exam_tip: The operator is responsible for ensuring maintenance is done — even if they hire someone else to do the actual work.
key_facts: Operator is responsible for maintaining aircraft in airworthy condition
Must follow approved maintenance programme
Maintenance must be done by certificated persons/organisations
Operator responsibility even when work is contracted out

syllabus_desc: State the requirement for annual and 100-hour inspections. CAR 91.
concept: Aircraft must undergo regular inspections to maintain airworthiness. The two main periodic inspections are: annual inspections (every 12 months) and 100-hour inspections (every 100 hours of flight time, required for aircraft used in flight training or hire). The 100-hour inspection can't be deferred — when you hit 100 hours since the last one, the aircraft is grounded until it's done. Annual inspections are calendar-based regardless of hours flown.
exam_tip: 100-hour inspections are required for training and hire aircraft. An annual inspection alone isn't enough for these operations.
key_facts: Annual inspection: every 12 calendar months
100-hour inspection: every 100 flight hours (for training/hire aircraft)
100-hour inspections cannot be deferred
Both must be performed by appropriately certificated persons

syllabus_desc: State the requirement for a review of airworthiness. CAR 91.
concept: A review of airworthiness is a comprehensive assessment of the aircraft's continuing fitness to fly. It's separate from routine inspections and looks at the big picture — is this aircraft still airworthy overall? The review examines maintenance records, compliance with airworthiness directives, the aircraft's modification history, and its general condition.
exam_tip: A review of airworthiness is more comprehensive than a routine inspection — it looks at the overall airworthiness status, not just current condition.
key_facts: Comprehensive assessment of continuing airworthiness
Examines maintenance records and compliance history
Checks compliance with all applicable airworthiness directives
Reviews modification history and general condition

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for maintenance records. CAR 91.
concept: Every piece of maintenance done on an aircraft must be recorded. The maintenance records must show what was done, who did it, when it was done, and any parts or components replaced. Records must be retained for the life of the aircraft (or component, as applicable). The technical log is the day-to-day record, while the maintenance logbooks provide the long-term history.
exam_tip: Maintenance records = lifetime retention. The technical log is the daily operational document; maintenance logbooks are the permanent record.
key_facts: All maintenance must be recorded
Records show: what, who, when, and parts replaced
Records retained for the life of the aircraft/component
Technical log: daily operational record
Maintenance logbooks: permanent history

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for and contents of a technical log. CAR 91.
concept: The technical log is the aircraft's day-to-day maintenance and serviceability record. It must be carried in the aircraft and must show the aircraft's current maintenance status, including any defects, deferred defects, and maintenance release information. Each flight should have the relevant details recorded. The technical log is required under rule 91.619 for every applicable aircraft.
exam_tip: The technical log travels WITH the aircraft. It shows current serviceability status and must include any known defects.
key_facts: Must be carried in the aircraft
Shows current maintenance/serviceability status
Records defects and deferred defects
Contains maintenance release information
Required under rule 91.619

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for entering defects into a technical log. CAR 91.
concept: When you find a defect — whether in flight or during a pre-flight inspection — it must be entered into the technical log. The entry should describe the defect clearly so a maintenance engineer can understand what's wrong. Defects that affect the aircraft's airworthiness must be rectified before further flight unless they can be properly deferred.
exam_tip: As PIC, if you notice something wrong, it goes in the tech log. If it affects airworthiness, the aircraft doesn't fly until it's fixed or properly deferred.
key_facts: All defects must be entered in the technical log
Description must be clear enough for maintenance action
Airworthiness-affecting defects must be rectified before flight
Some defects may be deferred under approved procedures
PIC has responsibility to report defects discovered in flight

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for clearing defects from a technical log. CAR 91.
concept: Only an appropriately certificated person can clear a defect from the technical log. The clearing entry must describe the corrective action taken and include the signature and certificate number of the person who did the work. You can't just cross out a defect and say 'she'll be right' — there must be a proper maintenance action and sign-off.
exam_tip: Only a certificated maintenance person can clear defects — not the pilot (unless they hold a pilot maintenance authorisation for limited items).
key_facts: Only certificated persons can clear defects
Clearing entry must describe corrective action taken
Must include signature and certificate number
Proper maintenance action required before clearing

syllabus_desc: State the limitations and requirements on a person undertaking 'pilot maintenance'. CAR 43.
concept: Pilot maintenance is limited maintenance that a qualified pilot can perform on their own aircraft without being a licensed engineer. Under Part 43, it covers simple tasks like oil changes, spark plug cleaning, tyre inflation, and similar minor items. You must follow the procedures in the aircraft maintenance manual, record the work in the maintenance documents, and you can only do it on aircraft you operate. You can't do pilot maintenance on someone else's aircraft or on anything complex.
exam_tip: Pilot maintenance = simple, routine tasks only. Follow the manual, record it properly, and don't exceed your authorisation.
key_facts: Limited to simple tasks: oil, spark plugs, tyres, etc.
Must follow aircraft maintenance manual procedures
Must record work in maintenance documents
Only on aircraft you operate
Cannot perform complex maintenance

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for conducting an operational flight check on an aircraft. CAR 91.
concept: After certain maintenance actions, an operational flight check (also called a maintenance test flight) may be required to verify the aircraft is performing correctly. This isn't a normal flight — it's conducted specifically to check that the maintenance work has been done correctly and the aircraft systems are functioning as intended. Specific procedures and limitations apply during these flights.
exam_tip: A maintenance test flight is not a normal operation — it has specific procedures, and passengers should not be carried.
key_facts: Required after certain maintenance actions
Conducted to verify maintenance work and system function
Specific procedures and limitations apply
Not a normal flight — different risk profile
Passengers should not be carried

syllabus_desc: State the inspection period for radios. CAR 91.
concept: Radio equipment must be inspected at regular intervals to ensure it's operating correctly and on the proper frequencies. The inspection period for radios is specified in the maintenance programme and applicable rules. Radio equipment inspections ensure your transmissions are on frequency, at the correct power, and that your receiver is working properly.
exam_tip: Radio inspections are periodic — check your aircraft's maintenance schedule for the specific interval.
key_facts: Radio equipment must be periodically inspected
Ensures correct frequency, power output, and receiver function
Inspection period specified in maintenance programme

syllabus_desc: State the inspection period for altimeters. CAR 91.
concept: Altimeters must be inspected and calibrated at regular intervals to ensure accuracy. An inaccurate altimeter is a serious safety hazard — if it reads 500 ft high, you're 500 ft closer to the ground than you think. The inspection period is specified in the maintenance programme.
exam_tip: Altimeter accuracy is critical for terrain clearance and separation from other aircraft. Regular calibration ensures the instrument is reading correctly.
key_facts: Altimeters must be periodically inspected and calibrated
Accuracy is critical for terrain clearance and separation
Inspection period specified in maintenance programme

syllabus_desc: State the inspection period for transponders. CAR 91.
concept: Transponders must be inspected and tested at regular intervals. Since transponders provide altitude and identification information to ATC and other aircraft (via SSR and ADS-B), their accuracy is safety-critical. An inaccurate transponder altitude report could mislead ATC about your actual level.
exam_tip: Transponder inspections ensure the altitude encoding is accurate and the identification codes are transmitting correctly.
key_facts: Transponders must be periodically inspected and tested
Accuracy critical for ATC surveillance and separation
Altitude encoding must be verified accurate
Inspection period per maintenance programme

syllabus_desc: State the normal inspection period for the ELT. CAR 91.
concept: ELTs must be inspected and tested periodically to ensure they'll actually work when you need them. This includes checking the battery expiry date, signal strength, and antenna condition. An ELT that doesn't work in a crash defeats its entire purpose. The battery must be replaced by the manufacturer's expiry date or after a cumulative use period, whichever comes first.
exam_tip: Check the ELT battery expiry date during your pre-flight. If it's expired, the ELT is considered unserviceable.
key_facts: ELT must be periodically inspected and tested
Battery expiry date must be checked
Signal strength and antenna condition verified
Battery replaced by expiry date or after cumulative use

syllabus_desc: State the minimum instrument requirements for a day VFR flight. CAR 91.
concept: Under rule 91.509, for a day VFR flight you need at minimum: an airspeed indicator, an altimeter (adjustable), a magnetic compass, a fuel quantity indicator for each fuel tank, an RPM indicator for each engine, an oil pressure indicator for each engine — plus additional instruments depending on the aircraft: coolant temperature (liquid-cooled), oil temperature (if oil cooler fitted), manifold pressure (if constant-speed prop or supercharged), CHT (if air-cooled), flap position indicator, landing gear indicator, and a means of checking electrical power if electrical systems are installed.
exam_tip: The basic six for day VFR powered flight: ASI, altimeter, compass, fuel gauge, RPM, oil pressure. Then add conditional instruments based on aircraft type.
key_facts: Airspeed indicator (ASI)
Altimeter (sensitive, adjustable)
Magnetic compass
Fuel quantity indicator for each tank
RPM indicator for each engine
Oil pressure indicator for each engine
Plus conditional: coolant temp, oil temp, manifold pressure, CHT, flap/gear indicators, electrical check

syllabus_desc: State the minimum instrument requirements for a night VFR flight. CAR 91.
concept: Night VFR adds to the day requirements. Under rule 91.511, in addition to everything you need for day VFR, you also need: a turn and slip indicator (or equivalent), position lights (nav lights), anti-collision lights, and a means of illuminating flight instruments. You're flying without natural horizon reference, so the turn and slip gives you attitude awareness, and the lights keep you visible and your instruments readable.
exam_tip: Night VFR = day VFR instruments PLUS turn and slip, position lights, anti-collision lights, and instrument illumination.
key_facts: All day VFR instruments plus:
Turn and slip indicator (or equivalent gyroscopic instrument)
Position lights (navigation lights)
Anti-collision lights
Means of illuminating flight instruments

syllabus_desc: State the radio equipment requirements for a VFR flight. CAR 91.
concept: Under rule 91.513, VFR flights in controlled airspace require level 1 or level 2 communications equipment. In uncontrolled airspace, radio isn't always required — but if you're operating in a mandatory broadcast zone or at a controlled aerodrome, you need it. The level of equipment determines what services you can access and what airspace you can enter.
exam_tip: No radio required in Class G uncontrolled airspace outside MBZs — but it's always good airmanship to have one. Controlled airspace = radio required.
key_facts: Level 1 or 2 comms equipment for VFR in controlled airspace
Radio required in mandatory broadcast zones (MBZs)
Radio required at controlled aerodromes
Not always required in uncontrolled Class G airspace
Equipment level determines accessible airspace/services

syllabus_desc: State the communications and navigation equipment requirements for a VFR over-water flight. CAR 91.
concept: Under rule 91.515, VFR flights over water have additional comms and nav equipment requirements beyond normal VFR. When you're over water and beyond gliding distance of shore, you need adequate communications equipment to maintain contact with an appropriate ATS unit, and navigation equipment appropriate to the route. The further over water you go, the more critical your equipment becomes.
exam_tip: Over water = extra equipment requirements. The key is maintaining communication with ATS and being able to navigate accurately when there are no ground references.
key_facts: Additional comms/nav equipment required for VFR over water
Must maintain contact with appropriate ATS unit
Navigation equipment appropriate to the route
Requirements increase with distance from shore

syllabus_desc: State the equipment requirements for a night VFR flight. CAR 91.
concept: For night VFR, in addition to the instruments already covered in 4.24.4, you need a torch (flashlight) available to each crew member — because if you lose electrical power, you'll need to read your instruments somehow. The aircraft must have serviceable position lights and anti-collision lights. You also need the night-specific instruments (turn and slip, instrument illumination).
exam_tip: Don't forget the torch! It's a legal requirement for night VFR under rule 91.221, not just a nice-to-have.
key_facts: Torch (flashlight) for each crew member
Serviceable position lights and anti-collision lights
Night-specific instruments per rule 91.511
Instrument illumination system
All day VFR equipment requirements also apply

syllabus_desc: State the equipment requirements for flight over water. CAR 91.
concept: Under rule 91.525, for flight over water, you need life preservers for every person on board when beyond gliding distance from shore. If you're in a single-engine aircraft beyond gliding distance, or a multi-engine aircraft that can't maintain 1,000 ft with an engine out, and you're more than 100 NM from shore — you need life rafts as well. Life preservers must be readily accessible (not buried in the baggage compartment).
exam_tip: Life preservers = anytime beyond gliding distance of shore. Life rafts = single-engine beyond glide distance OR multi-engine unable to maintain 1,000 ft on remaining engines, when >100 NM from shore.
key_facts: Life preservers: required beyond gliding distance from shore for all persons
Life rafts: single-engine beyond glide distance, or multi unable to maintain 1000 ft engine-out, >100 NM from shore
Life preservers must be readily accessible
Requirements in rule 91.525

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for indicating the time in flight. CAR 91.
concept: Under rule 91.221, you must carry a reliable timepiece that indicates hours, minutes, and seconds. In aviation, accurate time is critical for navigation, fuel calculations, position reporting, and SARTIME management. Your timepiece doesn't have to be a panel-mounted clock — a wristwatch is fine as long as it shows hours, minutes, and seconds reliably.
exam_tip: A timepiece with hours, minutes, AND seconds is required. Your phone in airplane mode with the clock visible works, but a watch on your wrist is more practical in the cockpit.
key_facts: Must carry a reliable timepiece showing hours, minutes, and seconds
Required under rule 91.221
Needed for navigation, fuel calculations, position reports, SARTIME
Can be any reliable timepiece (clock, watch, etc.)

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for emergency equipment in aircraft with seating capacity for less than 10 passengers. CAR 91.
concept: Under rule 91.523, aircraft with fewer than 10 passenger seats must carry: a first aid kit, and at least one hand fire extinguisher in the cockpit. The specific contents and quantities are prescribed in tables within the rule. For most light aircraft, this means a basic first aid kit and a halon or equivalent fire extinguisher accessible to the flight crew.
exam_tip: Small aircraft (< 10 pax seats) = first aid kit + fire extinguisher. These must be accessible, serviceable, and within inspection dates.
key_facts: First aid kit required
Hand fire extinguisher required in cockpit
Contents and quantities per Part 91 tables
Must be accessible and serviceable
Inspection dates must be current

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for an ELT. CAR 91.
concept: Under rule 91.529, aircraft must be equipped with an Aircraft Emergency Location System (AELS) or ELT, unless specifically exempted. The ELT must be approved by the Director and either automatically activated (ELT(AF) — permanently attached and auto-activates on impact) or survival type (ELT(S) — stowed for easy access and manually activated). The battery must be within its expiry date, and the ELT must be tested as per the maintenance programme.
exam_tip: Most light aircraft carry an ELT(AF) — automatic fixed, activates on impact. Know the difference between ELT(AF) and ELT(S).
key_facts: AELS or ELT required under rule 91.529
Must be approved by the Director
ELT(AF): automatic fixed — permanently attached, auto-activates on impact
ELT(S): survival — stowed for access, manually activated
Battery must be within expiry date
Must be tested per maintenance programme

syllabus_desc: Requirements of passengers to comply with instructions
concept: Passenger Compliance with Crew Instructions
exam_tip: CAR 91.103 — passengers must comply with all safety instructions given by the PIC or crew members, including safety briefing requirements.
key_facts: Passengers must follow all PIC and crew member instructions relating to safety
Failure to comply is an offense under CAR 91
Instructions cover safety briefing, restraint use, emergency procedures, and smoking
PIC has authority to enforce compliance before departure

syllabus_desc: Requirements for operating in simulated instrument flight
concept: Safety Pilot Requirements for Simulated Instrument Flight
exam_tip: CAR 91.125 — you must have a qualified safety pilot occupying a separate set of controls whenever you're practising instrument flight under the hood.
key_facts: Safety pilot must hold at least a Commercial Pilot Licence or higher
Safety pilot must have valid instrument rating
Safety pilot occupies a seat with full set of flight controls
Safety pilot maintains responsibility for aircraft safety and lookout
Practice must be in suitable airspace away from traffic

syllabus_desc: Requirements of PIC for safe operation
concept: PIC Responsibility for Safe Flight Operations
exam_tip: CAR 91.201 — as PIC, you're ultimately responsible for the safe operation of the aircraft. You must ensure the aircraft is airworthy and all required equipment is functioning.
key_facts: PIC holds final authority and responsibility for the flight
Must ensure aircraft is airworthy before flight
Must ensure all required equipment is operational
Must ensure crew members are qualified and briefed
Cannot knowingly operate an unsafe aircraft
Must ensure flight complies with all applicable rules

concept: PIC Authority to Refuse Carriage and Ensure Safety
exam_tip: CAR 91.203 — you can refuse to carry passengers or cargo if doing so would compromise safety, even if it causes inconvenience.
key_facts: PIC may refuse carriage of any person or cargo
May refuse passengers who are intoxicated or disruptive
May refuse baggage that is not properly restrained or dangerous
Refusal is lawful when based on safety grounds
PIC decision is final regarding safe operation
Can refuse to depart if weather or mechanical issues present risk

syllabus_desc: Crew members at stations, seats and safety belts
concept: Crew Station and Restraint Requirements
exam_tip: CAR 91.205 — all crew members must be at their assigned stations during takeoff, landing, and whenever the seatbelt sign is on.
key_facts: Crew must occupy designated crew stations during critical phases
Each crew member must have a seat with safety belt
Safety belt must be worn and properly fastened
Applies during all phases of flight when required
Crew cannot move about cabin during takeoff or landing

syllabus_desc: Occupation of seats and wearing of restraints
concept: Passenger Seating and Restraint Use Requirements
exam_tip: CAR 91.207 — all occupants must wear restraints during takeoff, landing, and when cruising below 1000 feet. Children under 4 years may be held by adults.
key_facts: Restraints required during takeoff and landing
Restraints required when cruising below 1000 feet
Restraints required during aerobatic flight
Restraints required in open cockpit aircraft at all times
Children under 4 may be held instead of using restraints
Helicopter requirements may differ — check POH

concept: Oxygen Requirements at Altitude
exam_tip: CAR 91.209 — in an unpressurised aircraft, you need oxygen if you're above 13,000 feet, or above 10,000 feet for longer than 30 minutes.
key_facts: Unpressurised aircraft: oxygen required above 13,000 feet continuously
Unpressurised aircraft: oxygen required above 10,000 feet if flight exceeds 30 minutes at that altitude
Pressurised aircraft has different rules based on cabin altitude
All occupants must have available oxygen supply
Oxygen must be supplied through masks or nasal cannulas
Check oxygen system before flight at high altitude

concept: Pre-Flight Safety Briefing Requirements
exam_tip: CAR 91.211 — you must brief all passengers on smoking, restraints, emergency procedures, exits, and any emergency equipment they might need.
key_facts: Brief before departure on all mandatory topics
Cover smoking ban and location of facilities
Explain restraint use and how to fasten/unfasten
Identify emergency exits and how to open them
Explain location of emergency equipment (life jackets, slides, etc.)
Explain oxygen use if required by altitude or duration
Inform passengers about emergency landing procedures
Advise on portable electronic device restrictions

syllabus_desc: Familiarity with operating limitations and emergency equipment
concept: Knowing Your Aircraft's Limits and Emergency Systems
exam_tip: CAR 91.219 — you must be familiar with the aircraft's operating limitations from the POH and know how to use all emergency equipment before flight.
key_facts: Study the Pilot's Operating Handbook before flying an unfamiliar aircraft
Understand weight and balance limitations
Know aircraft's performance limits in current conditions
Locate and understand all emergency equipment
Know how to operate fire extinguishers, first aid kits, oxygen systems
Understand emergency procedures specific to the aircraft
Be familiar with fuel system configuration and emergency procedures

concept: Required Charts and Publications for Flight
exam_tip: CAR 91.221 — you must carry all required aeronautical charts, publications, and operational information needed for your flight.
key_facts: Carry appropriate VNC or VFR charts covering entire route
Carry aerodrome charts for departure, destination, and alternates
Carry current NOTAMs (Notices to Airmen)
Carry AIP and AIP supplements relevant to the flight
Carry weather information and forecasts
Keep all documents current and updated before flight
Charts must cover minimum 5% beyond route boundaries

syllabus_desc: Operating on and in vicinity of aerodrome
concept: Aerodrome Operations and Circuit Procedures
exam_tip: CAR 91.127 — you must follow standard joining procedures, circuit patterns, and runway use rules. Don't make random approaches.
key_facts: Use standard overhead joining procedure unless instructed otherwise
Descend to circuit altitude outside the aerodrome traffic zone
Maintain circuit discipline — no shortcuts or low passes
Use designated runways unless ATC directs otherwise
Respect noise abatement procedures at controlled aerodromes
Give way to landing traffic
Maintain proper spacing from other aircraft in circuit

concept: Correct Technique for Joining the Aerodrome Circuit
exam_tip: AIP AD — the standard overhead join keeps you visible to other traffic and shows you respect the airspace. It's: arrive overhead, descend one side, turn downwind opposite runway.
key_facts: Arrive at aerodrome overhead at circuit altitude plus 1000 feet
Make a gentle descent on the non-landing side of runway
Turn downwind when abeam the runway
Maintain circuit altitude: normally 1000 feet AGL
Allows other traffic to see your intentions
Used when no other joining instructions given
Provides safe integration with circuit traffic

concept: Aircraft Priority and Collision Avoidance Rules
exam_tip: CAR 91.229 — remember: powered aircraft give way to balloons and gliders; converging aircraft: give way to the right; head-on: both turn right; overtaking: always turn right.
key_facts: Distressed aircraft has absolute priority — give way immediately
Aircraft landing has priority over all others
Gliders have priority over powered aircraft
Balloons have priority over all other aircraft
Converging at same altitude: aircraft on your right has priority
Head-on approach: both aircraft turn right
Overtaking: aircraft being overtaken has priority, turn right
Maintain visual lookout at all times
Priority rules don't override rules of collision avoidance

concept: Navigation Lights and Anti-Collision Lights
exam_tip: CAR 91.233 — you must display position lights at night (red/green/white), and anti-collision lights can be used day or night.
key_facts: Position lights (red/green wing lights, white tail light) required at night
Night begins 30 minutes after sunset, ends 30 minutes before sunrise
Anti-collision lights (red strobe) may be used any time
Lights must be operative before flight begins
Check light function during preflight
Some aircraft have landing lights as additional safety feature
Portable aircraft may have modified lighting requirements

concept: Carrying Identification While Flying
exam_tip: CAR 19.73 — always carry your pilot licence when flying. It's your proof of qualification and a legal requirement.
key_facts: Must carry valid pilot licence when exercising pilot privileges
Licence must be presented on demand to authority
Forgetting your licence means you're flying illegally
Licence must match the aircraft category and class
Medical certificate currency must be current
Carry licence in accessible location during flight

concept: Smoking Prohibition in Aircraft
exam_tip: CA Act 2023 S65N — smoking is prohibited in all aircraft in NZ airspace. No exceptions, no special areas.
key_facts: Smoking prohibited throughout the aircraft
Applies to all occupants including crew
No designated smoking areas in aircraft
Applies in all airspace (not just cruise)
Violation is an offense under the Act
Must brief passengers on smoking prohibition
Electronic cigarettes and vaping also prohibited

concept: Impairment Restrictions for Pilots and Crew
exam_tip: CAR 91.118 — you cannot fly if you're impaired by alcohol, drugs, or fatigue. 'Impaired' means any effect on performance, not just obvious intoxication.
key_facts: Cannot fly while under the influence of alcohol
Cannot fly if impaired by drugs (prescription or recreational)
Cannot fly if excessively fatigued
Impairment is judged by effect on performance, not intent
Eight-hour rule before flight recommended for alcohol
Passengers and crew must also comply
Violation is serious offense with heavy penalties

concept: Restrictions on PED Use During Flight
exam_tip: CAR 91 — PDAs and phones must be in airplane mode or switched off. Check POH and ATC instructions for each flight.
key_facts: Electronic devices capable of radio transmission must be in airplane mode
Devices that could interfere with navigation equipment must be switched off
Headsets and approved avionics are exempt
Crew devices may be used only when authorised
Passengers must follow crew instructions
Different restrictions may apply in different airspace

concept: Carriage of Firearms in Aircraft
exam_tip: CAR 91 — firearms are generally prohibited unless you have specific approval from the Authority. Check beforehand.
key_facts: Firearms prohibited unless specifically approved
Must obtain written permission from Director of Civil Aviation
Special procedures for authorised carriage
Ammunition also prohibited without approval
Different rules may apply for gliders and helicopters

concept: Baggage Stowage and Safety Requirements
exam_tip: CAR 91.213 — all baggage must be properly stowed and secured. Nothing can interfere with controls, exits, or emergency equipment.
key_facts: All baggage must be secured against movement
Nothing allowed in cockpit except required equipment
Baggage cannot block emergency exits
Cannot interfere with flight controls
Heavy items must be in aft baggage area if available
Weight limits must be respected for CG calculation

concept: Cargo Handling and Safety Procedures
exam_tip: CAR 91.215 — cargo must be loaded, secured, and distributed to maintain aircraft balance and prevent movement during flight.
key_facts: Cargo must be properly secured to prevent movement
Must not block emergency equipment or exits
Must be included in weight and balance calculation
Heavy cargo should be in aft cargo area when available
Cargo must not affect aircraft centre of gravity
Hazardous cargo has special restrictions

concept: Maintaining Safe Separation from Other Aircraft
exam_tip: CAR 91 — maintain adequate separation from all other aircraft. If you're getting close, you're too close. Use lookout and traffic awareness.
key_facts: Must maintain safe separation from all other aircraft
Adequate separation depends on altitude and activity
Cannot fly in formation without explicit agreement and planning
Use full-scale deflection on TCAS if available
Maintain visual contact with other aircraft
Report conflicting traffic to ATC if in controlled airspace

concept: Restrictions on Dropping Anything from the Aircraft
exam_tip: CAR 91.235 — you cannot drop anything from the aircraft unless you have specific approval (like spray operations or dropping cargo with parachutes).
key_facts: Dropping objects prohibited without written approval
Includes 'jettisoning' cargo in emergencies
Special approval needed for parachute operations
Special approval needed for aerial spraying
Agricultural operations have specific rules
Violating this rule endangers people below

concept: Maximum Airspeed Below 10,000 Feet
exam_tip: CAR 91.237 — you cannot fly faster than 250 knots below 10,000 feet. This is a hard limit, even in smooth air, even in a fast aircraft.
key_facts: Maximum 250 knots indicated airspeed below 10,000 feet MSL
Limit applies in all conditions and airspace
Designed to reduce collision risk in busy airspace
Your aircraft must be capable of flying slowly enough
Exception: training in own airspace may be slower than 250 knots
Above 10,000 feet, different limits may apply

concept: Minimum Safe Flying Heights
exam_tip: CAR 91.311 — in congested areas stay 1000 feet above the highest obstacle within 600 metres. Elsewhere, 500 feet above terrain or 150 metres horizontally from obstacles.
key_facts: Congested area: 1000 feet above highest obstacle within 600 m radius
Other areas: 500 feet above terrain OR 150 m horizontal from obstacles
Minimum height also applies to circuit patterns
Check local terrain and obstacles before flight
Some areas may have higher minimums — check charts
These are legal minimums — good practice is higher

concept: Flight into Known or Forecast Icing
exam_tip: CAR 91 — unless your aircraft is certified for icing and you have training, avoid flying into icing conditions. Icing kills.
key_facts: Cannot intentionally fly into known icing conditions without certification
Cessna 172s and most GA aircraft have no icing certification
Forecast icing should be avoided unless aircraft and training authorised
Icing accumulation dramatically affects performance and control
Check forecast freezing level and icing reports
Be prepared to divert if you encounter unexpected icing

concept: Limitations on Aerobatic Flight
exam_tip: CAR 91 — aerobatic flight requires specific clearance and is only allowed above 3000 feet AGL in designated areas away from populated areas.
key_facts: Aerobatic flight requires approved aircraft
Minimum 3000 feet AGL for aerobatic manoeuvres
Only in designated aerobatic areas
Away from populated areas and prohibited airspace
Pilot must have aerobatic endorsement
All occupants must wear parachutes

concept: Rules for Parachute Dropping from Aircraft
exam_tip: CAR 91 — parachute drops require specific approval and coordination with ATC. You need to establish a drop zone and maintain clear of other traffic.
key_facts: Parachute drops require written approval from Director
Must coordinate with ATC before operations
Establish designated drop zone clear of populated areas
Minimum altitude for deployment specified in approval
Maintain awareness of other traffic
Notify nearby aerodromes of drop operations
Drop zone must have ground safety officer

concept: Requirements for Towing Gliders Behind an Aircraft
exam_tip: CAR 91 — towing gliders requires special equipment, training, and procedures. The tow rope must be released if you enter emergency situations.
key_facts: Tow aircraft must have approved towing equipment
Pilot must have tow endorsement on licence
Glider pilot must be qualified and briefed
Rope release must be readily accessible to both pilots
Stay clear of populated areas during towing
Maintain smooth flying to prevent excessive stress on glider
Normal operating procedures may be restricted while towing

concept: Towing Banners, Targets, and Other Objects
exam_tip: CAR 91 — towing banners or targets requires approval and special procedures. Similar to glider towing but with different considerations for the object.
key_facts: Must have written approval from Director
Pilot must have appropriate endorsement
Object must be safely attached with redundant release
Stay clear of populated areas during towing
Avoid flying below 500 feet when towing
Object release must be operable from cockpit
Inspect object and attachment before each flight

concept: Minimum Weather Conditions for VFR Flight
exam_tip: CAR 91.301 Table 4 — remember the VFR minima: 5000m visibility and clear of cloud below 10000 feet, above that it's 8000m and 1000ft separation from cloud.
key_facts: Below 10,000 feet: 5000m visibility, clear of cloud
10,000-25,000 feet: 8000m visibility, 1000ft above/below cloud
Above 25,000 feet: 8000m visibility, 1000ft above, 1500ft below cloud
Must see ahead to avoid terrain and obstacles
Meteorological visibility must not be confused with flight visibility
Check latest weather before departure
Turn back or divert if conditions deteriorate below minima

syllabus_desc: Special VFR restrictions and met minima
concept: SVFR Conditions and Requirements
exam_tip: CAR 91.303 — Special VFR allows you to fly VFR in controlled airspace below 1000 feet with reduced minima, but only in daytime with 1500m visibility and clear of cloud.
key_facts: SVFR available in controlled airspace only
Minimum visibility 1500m in level flight, 600m forward
Minimum 600 feet above terrain
Clear of cloud requirement applies
Daytime operations only
ATC clearance required
Designed for airfield approaches in poor weather

syllabus_desc: Restrictions on carriage of dangerous goods
concept: What You Cannot Carry on the Aircraft
exam_tip: CAR 92 — dangerous goods are prohibited in aircraft except for specific exemptions. When in doubt, don't carry it. Check the ICAO Dangerous Goods Regulations.
key_facts: Most explosives, flammables, toxics, and corrosives prohibited
Limited exceptions for OPA (Operator's Personal Allowance)
Batteries, lighters, and some chemicals have specific rules
Training required to authorise certain goods
Packaging and labelling required when allowed
Penalties for carrying prohibited goods are severe
Passengers responsible for declaring dangerous goods

syllabus_desc: Requirements for carriage of non-dangerous goods
concept: Safe Transport of Baggage and Cargo
exam_tip: CAR 92 — non-dangerous cargo must be properly stowed, secured, and declared. The aircraft operator is responsible for what's on board.
key_facts: All baggage must be secure against movement in flight
Cargo must not affect weight and balance
Cargo must not block emergency exits or equipment
Fragile items must be properly cushioned
Live animals have special requirements
Perishable goods must be temperature controlled if needed
Owner's responsibility extends to what's in their baggage

concept: Getting the Right Information Before Flight
exam_tip: CAR 91.217 — before every flight, check weather, fuel, alternates, traffic, comms/nav, aerodrome info, and aircraft performance. If you skip any of this, you're not ready.
key_facts: Check latest meteorological forecasts and reports
Verify fuel availability at destination and alternates
Identify suitable alternate aerodromes
Check for traffic delays and congestion
Verify communication and navigation facilities available
Check aerodrome operational status (NOTAMs, closures)
Calculate aircraft performance for current conditions
Review any special procedures or restrictions

syllabus_desc: Publications and their content for operational info
concept: Using AIP, Charts, and Publications for Planning
exam_tip: AIP and supplements contain frequencies, procedures, lighting info, and special restrictions. Get familiar with these publications — they're your reference library.
key_facts: AIP provides permanent and semi-permanent aviation information
AIP Supplements contain temporary changes
NOTAMs provide short-notice operational changes
VNC charts show terrain, airspace, reporting points, and aerodromes
