Aerodrome charts show runways, taxiways, and facilities
En Route charts show airways, reporting points, and frequencies
All publications must be current before flight

syllabus_desc: Derive operational information from charts and publications
concept: Reading Charts and Understanding Operational Information
exam_tip: Spend time learning to read your charts properly. Understand symbols, colours, numbers, and how to find runways, frequencies, and obstacles.
key_facts: Colour coding shows airspace classification
Heights on charts show terrain elevation and obstacle heights
Frequencies are listed for each aerodrome and reporting point
Symbols show airfield facilities and hazards
MOAs, restricted areas, and danger areas clearly marked
Radio coverage may be limited in mountainous areas
Practice extracting information before flight

concept: Fuel Required for Daytime VFR Flight
exam_tip: CAR 91.305 — minimum fuel is: enough to reach destination, plus 30 minutes more at normal cruising power. For helicopters add 20 minutes instead.
key_facts: Aeroplane: fuel to destination + 30 minutes reserve
Helicopter: fuel to destination + 20 minutes reserve
Calculate at normal cruising power settings
Must account for headwinds and enroute diversion
Don't push to the legal minimum — safety margin essential
Check fuel consumption before departure
Verify fuel on board matches calculation

concept: Fuel Required for Night VFR Flight
exam_tip: CAR 91.305 — night requires more fuel: destination plus 45 minutes reserve (not 30). Night operations are more demanding and options are limited.
key_facts: Aeroplane: fuel to destination + 45 minutes reserve
Night defined as 30 min after sunset to 30 min before sunrise
Calculate at normal cruising power settings
Diversion more difficult in darkness — extra reserve important
Check alternate aerodromes have lights available
Don't plan to arrive with minimal fuel at night
Weather deterioration is harder to handle in darkness

concept: When to File and How VFR Flight Plans Work
exam_tip: CAR 91.307 — file a flight plan if flying more than 50NM from shore, or if you want search-and-rescue alerting. VFR flight plan activates when you're airborne.
key_facts: Flight plan required: >50NM from shore
Flight plan optional but recommended: close to shore
File at aerodrome or via radio if airborne
Provide estimated time en route (ETE)
Activation occurs on crossing aerodrome boundary
Includes SAR alerting if you don't close flight plan
Check flight plan details before filing

syllabus_desc: Notification of changes to flight plan
concept: Amending Your Flight Plan in Flight
exam_tip: CAR 91 — if your plans change en route (diverting, changed ETA, different routing), notify ATC or flight service early. Don't just show up somewhere unexpected.
key_facts: Notify ATC of significant changes immediately
Report revised ETA if delayed or ahead of schedule
Report diversions due to weather or mechanical issues
Don't change routing without checking restrictions first
Give ATC minimum 15 minutes notice of major changes
Continue to report position if required

concept: Closing Your Flight Plan on Arrival
exam_tip: CAR 91 — close your flight plan within 30 minutes of landing. If you don't, SAR gets activated and you might get a bill for unnecessary search operations.
key_facts: Close flight plan within 30 minutes of landing
Can close via ATC, flight service, or aerodrome operator
SAR activation triggered 30 minutes after ETA if not closed
Late closure penalties possible if SAR activated
Phone closure acceptable at remote aerodromes
Always close — don't rely on 'they'll know I landed'

syllabus_desc: Difference between ETA and SAR time (SARTIME)
concept: Understanding When Search and Rescue Activates
exam_tip: Your ETA is when you expect to land; SARTIME is the ETA plus 30 minutes. If you don't close the flight plan by SARTIME, SAR starts looking for you.
key_facts: ETA: Estimated Time of Arrival at destination
SARTIME: ETA plus 30 minutes
SAR automatically activated at SARTIME if flight plan open
Close flight plan before SARTIME
Late closure may result in SAR response
Costs of unnecessary SAR activation may be recoverable

syllabus_desc: Time SAR is initiated if flight plan not terminated
concept: SAR Activation Timeline
exam_tip: AIP ENR — SAR kicks in 30 minutes after your ETA if you haven't closed the flight plan. That's SARTIME. Don't be late closing.
key_facts: SAR initiates at SARTIME (ETA + 30 minutes)
Initial SAR action: attempt to contact pilot on known frequency
If no contact: activate initial search phase
Search area expands as time passes
Full SAR response activated if no indication of safety
Pilot responsible for timely flight plan closure

concept: Finding the Right Frequency for Each Flight Segment
exam_tip: Use the AIP and your chart legend to locate frequencies. Each aerodrome has tower or CTAF, each reporting point has a frequency, and airways have radio ranges listed.
key_facts: Tower frequency listed on aerodrome chart
CTAF or traffic frequency posted at uncontrolled aerodromes
Position reporting frequencies on en route chart
Airways have primary and secondary frequencies
Frequency coverage maps show communication range
Check current NOTAM for frequency changes
Have backup frequency if primary unavailable

concept: Reporting Your Position to Air Traffic Services
exam_tip: CAR 91.309 — in controlled airspace you must report at specific reporting points. Position reports let ATC track you and provide separation.
key_facts: Report at each mandatory reporting point in controlled airspace
Report at intervals specified by ATC (usually 30 min)
First report at aerodrome boundary when filing VFR
Position report must be concise but complete
Include: callsign, position, altitude, heading, ETA
Mandatory reporting points marked on chart

concept: What to Say in a Position Report
exam_tip: AIP ENR — a proper position report: callsign, position (landmark or reporting point), altitude, heading, next reporting point, ETA. Keep it professional and brief.
key_facts: Aircraft callsign (full, then abbreviated)
Position (reporting point name or visual reference)
Flight level or altitude (FL or feet MSL)
Heading (magnetic)
Next reporting point
Estimated time at next point
Any other relevant traffic or weather info

concept: Using UNICOM for Aerodrome Information and Traffic
exam_tip: AIP GEN — UNICOM is an uncontrolled frequency (often 118.2 or 119.0) where pilots exchange traffic and weather info with other aircraft and the aerodrome operator.
key_facts: UNICOM transmits on 118.2 or 119.0 (varies by aerodrome)
Used at uncontrolled aerodromes for traffic coordination
Allows pilots to hear others and share traffic positions
Aerodrome operator may monitor for essential messages
No ATC separation provided on UNICOM
Pilots retain full responsibility for separation
Common at rural NZ aerodromes

concept: Aerodrome Flight Radio Unit for Non-Controlled Aerodromes
exam_tip: AIP GEN — AFRU is a radio station at some aerodromes that relays messages and provides information. It doesn't separate traffic like ATC does.
key_facts: AFRU operates at uncontrolled aerodromes
Relays messages between pilots and aerodrome operator
Provides weather updates and aerodrome information
Does not provide ATC separation or clearance
Typically monitors 119.0 or local frequency
Operates reduced hours at some smaller aerodromes
Not a control tower — just communication aid

concept: Aerodrome Weather Information Broadcast
exam_tip: AIP GEN — AWIB transmits automated weather reports on the aerodrome UNICOM frequency. It's recorded so you can get current conditions anytime.
key_facts: AWIB broadcasts automated weather information
Transmitted on UNICOM frequency, usually 118.2 or 119.0
Provides wind, temperature, dew point, QNH, visibility
Usually updated every 30 minutes automatically
Recorded transmission available on demand
Supplements aerodrome ATIS where available
Essential information for approach planning

concept: Understanding ATC Signals When Radio Fails
exam_tip: CAR 91.243; AIP AD Table 1 — if your radio fails, ATC uses light signals. Red light = don't land; green light = clear to land. White = go to aerodrome or land when ready.
key_facts: Green steady: cleared to land or taxi
Red steady: don't land, don't approach
Red flashing: aerodrome unsafe, don't land
White flashing: go to aerodrome, landing not advised
Green flashing: cleared to take off
White steady: land (from air) or clear to proceed (on ground)
Understand signal meanings before flying

concept: Two-way In-Flight Broadcast (TIBA) Procedures
exam_tip: AIP ENR — TIBA lets you broadcast position and intentions without two-way radio contact. Listen to the frequency and broadcast in the blind.
key_facts: TIBA used when no ATC available
Broadcast position, altitude, heading on assigned frequency
Listen for other aircraft broadcasts
Acknowledge other aircraft position reports
Provides mutual awareness in uncontrolled airspace
No separation provided — maintain visual lookout
Typical frequency 119.0 or local area frequency

syllabus_desc: Complying with ATC clearances and instructions
concept: Following ATC Instructions and Clearances
exam_tip: CAR 91.241 — once you receive a clearance from ATC, you must comply with it. If you can't, say so immediately. Don't just ignore it.
key_facts: ATC clearances are mandatory when issued
Clearance is authorisation to proceed under stated conditions
If you can't comply, say 'unable' immediately
Read back critical clearances (altitude, heading, etc.)
Maintain assigned altitude and heading
Report any significant deviation or problem
Emergency is exception — declare emergency if needed

concept: Working with Aerodrome Flight Information Service
exam_tip: CAR 91 — AFIS provides information and traffic awareness at uncontrolled aerodromes, but doesn't issue clearances like a control tower does.
key_facts: AFIS provides traffic information, not separation
You remain responsible for your own separation
AFIS may suggest runway or traffic pattern
Maintains continuous watch on operations
Reports weather and runway conditions
Available at busier uncontrolled aerodromes
Doesn't have authority to issue clearances

syllabus_desc: ATC clearance for entering various airspace
concept: Getting Clearance to Enter Controlled Airspace
exam_tip: CAR 91.245; AIP ENR — you need an ATC clearance before entering CTRs and some CTA. VFR flight plan provides basic entry, but specific clearance may be required.
key_facts: CTR (Control Zone): clearance required
CTA (Control Area): clearance required above certain levels
VFR flight plan activates automatically at boundary
Request clearance 10-15 minutes before CTR boundary
State altitude, position, destination when requesting
Clearance includes route, altitude, and special restrictions
Don't enter without clearance — it's airspace violation

syllabus_desc: ATC clearance for re-entering controlled airspace
concept: Re-Entry Procedures After Leaving Controlled Airspace
exam_tip: CAR 91.245; AIP ENR — if you've exited a CTR or CTA and want to come back in, request a re-entry clearance from ATC before you cross the boundary.
key_facts: Request re-entry clearance before re-crossing boundary
Clearance confirms ATC is aware of your return
Position should be clear of airspace when requesting
Provide current position, altitude, and destination
ATC will issue clearance with specific instructions
Don't assume you can just come back in
Re-entry protects against conflicts with other traffic

concept: Using Clock Positions to Describe Traffic Location
exam_tip: In radio calls, 12 o'clock is straight ahead, 3 o'clock is right wing, 6 o'clock is behind, 9 o'clock is left wing. Always estimate range too.
key_facts: 12 o'clock: directly ahead
3 o'clock: right 90 degrees
6 o'clock: directly behind
9 o'clock: left 90 degrees
Intermediate positions between these reference points
Always include altitude and range
Example: 'traffic two o'clock, 2 miles, 500 feet above'

syllabus_desc: ATC responsible for separation (VFR, SVFR, IFR)
concept: When ATC Provides Separation Service
exam_tip: AIP ENR — ATC provides separation for IFR, SVFR in controlled airspace, and VFR in Class C and D airspace. Outside that, you're responsible for your own separation.
key_facts: IFR in controlled airspace: full ATC separation
SVFR in controlled airspace: full ATC separation
VFR in Class C/D: ATC separation from IFR
VFR in Class E/F: no ATC separation provided
Outside controlled airspace: pilot responsible
VFR pilots must maintain own visual separation
Traffic information provided where possible

concept: Your Responsibility to Avoid Other Aircraft
exam_tip: AIP ENR — as PIC, you're always responsible for seeing and avoiding other aircraft, even when in controlled airspace. ATC helps, but the final decision is yours.
key_facts: PIC maintains ultimate responsibility for separation
Visual lookout is primary method
Use traffic information from ATC as aid
Traffic advisories help but don't guarantee separation
Right-of-way rules apply even with ATC radar
Be prepared to manoeuvre if separation reduced
Don't assume ATC has seen all traffic

concept: Standard Separation Distances ATC Uses
exam_tip: AIP ENR — ATC uses different separation standards depending on altitude and airspace. Vertical separation is typically 1000 feet; horizontal varies.
key_facts: Vertical separation (IFR): typically 1000 feet below FL250, 2000 feet above
Horizontal separation: 5-10 nautical miles depending on airspace
Reduced separation possible with modern radar
Separation standards increase in lower altitude airspace
VFR separation may be less stringent than IFR
Wake turbulence separation greater for heavy aircraft

concept: When Separation Standards Are Less Stringent
exam_tip: AIP ENR — in some situations, ATC can apply reduced separation if radar coverage is good and pilots understand. This speeds things up but requires precise flying.
key_facts: RVSM: Reduced Vertical Separation Minima (2000 feet above FL290)
Requires special approval and equipment
Modern radar allows closer spacing in busy airspace
Pilots must be more precise with altitude adherence
Wake turbulence separation may be closer for known aircraft types
Approval required before operating in RVSM airspace

syllabus_desc: Wake turbulence separation for light aircraft
concept: Staying Clear of Vortex Trails from Larger Aircraft
exam_tip: AIP AD — large aircraft create dangerous wake vortices. Stay at least 2 miles behind and 1000 feet above or below them, more if you're slower.
key_facts: Heavy aircraft: B747, A380, A350, B777, A340
Vortices extend 5-10 nautical miles behind aircraft
Strength peaks 1000-2000 feet below aircraft level
Vertical separation: maintain 1000 feet above or below
Horizontal separation: 2-3 nautical miles behind
Crossing traffic: cross above and beyond vortex cores
Particularly hazardous near aerodromes (approach, landing)

concept: Traffic Advisory and Radar Information Services
exam_tip: AIP ENR — radar services include traffic advisories and information. ATC provides vectors for separation or to avoid weather, but you maintain separation responsibility.
key_facts: Traffic information: ATC advises of traffic radar sees
Radar vectors: heading to ensure separation
Approach service: radar guidance to aerodrome
Traffic avoidance advisories help but don't guarantee safety
Your responsibility for separation remains
Radar coverage may be limited in mountainous areas
Request radar services from ATC when available

concept: Setting Your Altimeter for Flight at Different Levels
exam_tip: CAR 91.239; AIP ENR — below 13,000 feet use area QNH; 13,000-FL150 use transition level QNH; above FL150 use 1013.2 hPa (standard).
key_facts: Below 13,000 feet: set area QNH
13,000-FL150: use QNH as advised by ATC
FL150 and above: set 1013.2 hPa (Standard)
QNH obtained from aerodrome or ATC
Always cross-check altimeter setting with known height
Different QNH areas on charts
Transition level/altitude on approach charts

concept: Flying When You Can't Get Current QNH
exam_tip: AIP ENR — if QNH unavailable, estimate altimeter setting from terrain elevation or use a nearby reporting aerodrome. Cross-check with landmarks.
key_facts: Request QNH from all available sources first
Last known QNH can be used with caution
Estimate from nearby aerodromes or weather
Reduce altitude to cross-check against terrain
Accept radar altimeter information if available
Be extra vigilant for terrain clearance
Document what setting you used

concept: Different Altimeter Settings for Different Regions
exam_tip: AIP ENR — NZ has several QNH zones. Your chart shows boundaries. Set the correct zone QNH when you enter a new zone.
key_facts: QNH varies by geographical region
Chart shows QNH zone boundaries
Get new QNH when entering new zone
Each zone has primary reporting aerodrome
QNH obtained from aerodrome or ATC
May change during flight due to weather systems
Check NOTAM for QNH changes

concept: Correct Altitude to Cruise at for Your Heading
exam_tip: CAR 91.313; AIP ENR — above 3000 feet AMSL or 1000 feet AGL: fly odd+500 feet if heading 270-089°, even+500 if heading 090-269°.
key_facts: 270-089° heading: fly odd thousands + 500 (3500, 5500, 7500, etc.)
090-269° heading: fly even thousands + 500 (4500, 6500, 8500, etc.)
Applies above 3000 feet AMSL or 1000 feet AGL
Improves separation between opposite direction traffic
Reduces head-on collision risk
Standard in uncontrolled airspace
ATC may assign different level in controlled airspace

concept: When to Ignore the Standard Rules
exam_tip: AIP ENR — if ATC assigns a non-standard altitude (not odd/even+500), you must fly it. ATC has radar and knows what's coming.
key_facts: ATC can assign any altitude for separation
Must comply with assigned altitude
Maintains separation from other traffic
Radar vectoring may require different level
Read back assigned altitude to confirm
Maintain assigned altitude within limits

concept: Mode C Transponder Codes and Operations
exam_tip: CAR 91.247; AIP ENR — default VFR code is 1200, gliders 1300, controlled aerodrome circuit 2200, IFR 2000. Use assigned codes in controlled airspace.
key_facts: VFR: code 1200 (always-own)
Gliders: code 1300
Controlled aerodrome circuit: code 2200
IFR: code 2000 initially, then assigned
Turn on transponder at ground
Mode A (code only) or Mode C (code + altitude)
Ident for 5 seconds when requested

concept: How to Use Your Transponder Correctly
exam_tip: AIP ENR — turn on before takeoff, select correct code, press ident when asked, maintain continuous operation during flight, turn off after landing.
key_facts: Select Mode C (altitude reporting) when possible
Set correct code before entering controlled airspace
Keep transponder on throughout flight
Press ident button when requested by ATC
Monitor transponder operation during flight
Turn off transponder after landing
Check mode and code before each flight

concept: Operating VFR without Transponder in Terminal Manoeuvring Zone
exam_tip: CAR 91.247; AIP ENR — you can't fly VFR in a TMZ without a serviceable Mode C transponder. It's a rule to increase safety in busy terminal airspace.
key_facts: TMZ requires Mode C transponder for VFR
Unpermitted without transponder (non-emergency)
Emergency procedures may override
Check NOTAMs for TMZ boundaries
Failure to comply is airspace violation
Non-compliance may result in enforcement action

syllabus_desc: VFR rules in various airspace classes
concept: What You Can Do in Each Airspace Class
exam_tip: CAR 91; AIP ENR — Class A (IFR only), B/C/D (mixed), E (VFR allowed), F (VFR), G (uncontrolled). Check each chart for specific rules.
key_facts: Class A: IFR only — VFR not permitted
Class B: VFR allowed below certain level
Class C/D: VFR allowed with clearance
Class E: VFR allowed with restrictions
Class F: VFR, no restrictions on entry
Class G: uncontrolled, no clearance needed
Check chart legend for each airspace definition

concept: Control Zones and Their Boundaries
exam_tip: CAR 71; AIP ENR — CTRs (Control Zones) surround busy aerodromes. They protect departure and approach corridors. You need clearance to enter.
key_facts: CTR surrounds controlled aerodrome
Typical radius 5-10 NM depending on traffic
Vertical extent varies: usually surface to 3000-5000 feet
Purpose: separate approaching/departing traffic
Clearance required for entry (VFR and IFR)
Flight plan activates entry clearance
Boundaries shown on charts as blue dashed lines

concept: Control Areas and En Route Airspace
exam_tip: CAR 71; AIP ENR — CTAs (Control Areas) are blocks of controlled airspace from surface to high altitude, protecting airways. Clearance required above certain level.
key_facts: CTA provides airway traffic separation
Vertical extent from surface to FL245 or higher
Clearance required for IFR operations
VFR may transit under specified conditions
Purpose: separate en route traffic
Boundaries shown on charts as blue lines
Airways lie within CTA

concept: Designated Routes Through Controlled Airspace
exam_tip: AIP ENR — VFR transit lanes allow VFR through controlled airspace without IFR flight plan. Follow the specified route and altitude for safe transit.
key_facts: Transit lanes route VFR through CTA
Specific altitude bands for transit
Less restrictive than full ATC clearance
Published on en route charts
Provides VFR access to airspace
Reduces ATC workload for routine traffic
Must maintain published altitude and route

concept: Uncontrolled Airspace for General Aviation
exam_tip: CAR 91.139; AIP ENR — GAAs are zones of uncontrolled airspace for VFR operations. No clearance needed, but you must follow VFR rules and conduct position broadcasts.
key_facts: GAA: uncontrolled airspace designated for GA
VFR flight allowed without clearance
Broadcast position on UNICOM frequency
No ATC separation provided
Maintain VFR weather minima
Height limits vary by GAA
Boundaries marked on VNC chart

concept: Landmarks and Navigation Checkpoints
exam_tip: AIP ENR & VNC charts — VRPs are distinctive landmarks (towers, towns, lakes) used for position reporting. They're shown on your chart with symbols.
key_facts: VRPs: easily identifiable from air
Used for position reporting
Shown on VNC and approach charts
Symbol shows exact location
Named and numbered for reference
Useful for dead reckoning navigation
Report position relative to VRP if needed

syllabus_desc: Controlled airspace when ATC off duty
concept: What Happens When the Tower Closes
exam_tip: AIP GEN — when ATC is off duty, the CTR becomes uncontrolled airspace (usually Class F). You follow uncontrolled airspace rules but must use the CTAF.
key_facts: CTR converts to uncontrolled status when ATC closes
Become Class F or Class G (check NOTAMs)

concept: Airspace Off-Limits for General Aviation

key_facts: Restricted areas marked on charts (R-series)

Check NOTAMs for active hours
Don't enter without clearance if active

Vertical limits specified for each area

concept: Airspace Where Military Aircraft Conduct Training
exam_tip: CAR 91; AIP ENR — MOAs are active airspace where military conducts high-performance flying. Transit caution advised; check NOTAMs for activation.

concept: Broadcasting Your Position on Local Frequency
exam_tip: CAR 91.135; AIP ENR — MBZs require you to broadcast position, altitude, and intentions on the designated frequency to alert other traffic.

exam_tip: CAR 91.131; AIP ENR — LFZs restrict flight below certain heights to protect training areas or special operations. Check your chart for LFZ boundaries.

concept: Designated Frequency for Uncontrolled Airspace
exam_tip: AIP ENR — CFZs use a common frequency (usually 119.0) for traffic coordination. All pilots in the zone broadcast position regularly.

concept: Changes to Airspace Due to Events or Hazards
exam_tip: AIP Supplement and NOTAMs — temporary restrictions (fireworks, air shows, weather events) are published as NOTAMs. Always check before each flight.

concept: Reading Airspace Symbols and Boundaries
exam_tip: Chart Legend — learn the symbols. Blue shading = controlled, orange = restricted, green = military, red = danger. Boundaries shown with specific line styles.

Orange hatching: restricted area

concept: Where You Can and Cannot Land
exam_tip: CAR 91.127 — you can only land at aerodromes certified for public use unless you have special permission. Private property airstrips require owner consent.

Private strips: owner approval required

concept: Understanding Runway Numbers
exam_tip: AIP AD — runways numbered by magnetic heading rounded to nearest 10°. 09 = roughly 090°, 27 = roughly 270°. Parallel runways get L/C/R suffixes.
key_facts: Runway 09: heading roughly 090° (East)
Runway 18: heading roughly 180° (South)
Runway 27: heading roughly 270° (West)
Runway 36: heading roughly 360°/000° (North)
Parallel runways: Left (L), Centre (C), Right (R)
Number visible on runway from air
Reciprocal runway on opposite end

concept: Runway, Taxiway, and Apron Distinctions
exam_tip: CAR 1 — movement area = runways + taxiways. You need ATC clearance before entering at controlled aerodromes. Be careful taxiing.
key_facts: Runway: take-off and landing area
Taxiway: path from runway to apron
Apron: parking area for aircraft

Follow taxiway markings and signs
Hold short of runways when instructed
Avoid other aircraft and ground vehicles

concept: Lights and Markings on the Ground
exam_tip: AIP AD — various lights show runway alignment, elevation, and hazards. Look for green approach lights, red/white threshold lights, etc.
key_facts: Runway edge lights: white (or amber near end)

concept: Reading Aerodrome Layout Diagrams
exam_tip: AIP GEN Vol 4 — aerodrome chart shows runways, taxiways, apron, fuel, hangers, and facilities. Before you land, know where you're going and how to taxi there.

Taxiways named with letters (A, B, C)
Apron area for parking and ground operations
Fuel, maintenance, and terminal locations marked
Parking areas for visiting aircraft
Runway length shown (critical for performance)
Threshold, hold-short lines marked

syllabus_desc: Runway and taxiway signs and markings
concept: Understanding Airport Signage
exam_tip: CAR 139 — runway distance remaining signs (white on black) count down distance. Hold-short lines are thick white lines. Taxiway centerlines are yellow.
key_facts: Distance remaining signs: white numbers on black
Hold-short line: thick white line (don't cross without clearance)
Taxiway centerline: yellow line
Taxiway edge line: yellow or white

syllabus_desc: Lighting systems (REDL, RTHL, RENL, RCLL, REIL, CGL, RLLS, PAL)

exam_tip: Understand the basics: edge lights (REDL), threshold (RTHL), end (RENL), centerline (RCLL), approach (CGL, PAL), intensity lights (REIL), overrun lights (RLLS).
key_facts: REDL: Runway Edge Lights (white, or amber near end)
RTHL: Runway Threshold Lights (green at start)
RENL: Runway End Lights (red at end)

concept: Airport Rotating Lights for Identification
exam_tip: Aerodromes have rotating beacons (green/white or red/white) on top of control tower or building. Flashing pattern identifies the aerodrome at night.

White/green: civilian/public aerodrome
Red/white: civilian aerodrome with obstacles
Flash pattern helps identify aerodrome
Visible from distance at night
Indicates aerodrome location
Active even when tower closed

concept: Reporting Aircraft Accidents to Authorities
exam_tip: CAR 12 rule 12.51 — if you have an accident, notify the Director ASAP (same day). Provide 14 data items, then full report within 10 days.

Report includes 14 standard data items
Full report required within 10 days
Preserve aircraft and evidence for investigation
Cooperate fully with investigators
Recording statements may help (legal advice first)

concept: Reporting Less Serious Aircraft Incidents
exam_tip: CAR 12 rule 12.55 — incidents (near misses, technical faults, medical events) must be reported. Details within 14 days.
key_facts: Incident: problem not reaching accident threshold

Report to Director within 14 days
Detailed written account required
Investigation may occur

Honest reporting helps improve aviation safety

syllabus_desc: Extent of deviation from rules in emergency
concept: Breaking Rules When Life Is at Risk
exam_tip: CA Act 2023 S15 — in genuine emergency, you can ignore rules if danger to life/property justifies it. The emergency must be greater than the rule violation risk.
key_facts: Emergency deviation allowed when immediate danger exists
Examples: engine failure, medical emergency, fire
Action must be the only way to save life/property
Must minimize collateral damage
Can't be used for convenience or minor problems
Declare emergency to ATC

concept: Reporting Rule Violations Made in Emergency
exam_tip: CA Act 2023 S15 — after emergency deviation, notify ATC immediately and notify Director ASAP. Failure to notify = $15,000 fine.

Describe what made emergency necessary

concept: Declaring Emergency on Transponder
exam_tip: AIP ENR — if you have an emergency (engine failure, medical, structural damage), squawk 7700 on your transponder and declare emergency on radio.

Examples: engine failure, fire, medical, structural damage
Squawk 7700 and say 'MAYDAY'

exam_tip: AIP ENR — if your radio fails, squawk 7600 to tell ATC. They'll monitor your transponder and use light signals to guide you.

Maintain assigned altitude and heading
Proceed to nearest suitable aerodrome
Expect light signals (green = clear to land)

concept: If the Aircraft Is Hijacked
exam_tip: AIP ENR — if someone forces you to divert or threatens crew, squawk 7500. This alerts authorities immediately without alerting the hijacker.

syllabus_desc: ATC verification of emergency SSR code

exam_tip: AIP ENR — when ATC sees 7700 or 7600, they'll ask you to verify (squawk ident or 'say your emergency'). Confirm your situation for priority handling.
